course_info.title: Psychology Field Experience
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday - 1 hour seminar plus 8 hours per week at field placement

class_schedule.topic: Preparing for the Field: Professional Conduct and Expectations
learning_objectives: Describe the roles and responsibilities of a psychology practicum student
Identify ethical obligations in applied settings: confidentiality, boundaries, scope of practice
Develop professional goals for the field placement
readings: APA Ethics Code (review); field placement handbook

lecture_notes.introduction: The field experience bridges academic knowledge and professional practice. You will apply classroom learning in a real-world mental health or research setting under supervision.
key_concepts: Professional conduct: punctuality, dress, communication, initiative
Confidentiality in practice: HIPAA compliance, chart notes, incidental disclosures
Scope of practice: what you can and cannot do as a student
Supervision: preparing for supervision sessions, receiving feedback
Self-care and vicarious trauma prevention
lecture_notes.demonstration: Role-play: practice introducing yourself to a new site supervisor and asking about expectations

review.value: As a practicum student, you observe and assist under supervision. You do not conduct independent assessments, provide therapy without supervision, or make diagnostic decisions.
Come to supervision with specific questions, cases to discuss, and honest reflections on challenges. Supervision is where the most learning occurs.

learning_objectives: Practice systematic behavioral observation in clinical settings
Learn to write professional progress notes (SOAP format)
Describe the purpose and structure of clinical documentation
readings: Cameron & Turtle-Song, 'Learning to Write Case Notes' (The Clinical Supervisor)

lecture_notes.introduction: Clinical documentation is a core professional skill. Clear, accurate, and timely notes are legal documents that communicate across the treatment team.
key_concepts: SOAP note format: Subjective, Objective, Assessment, Plan
DAP note format: Data, Assessment, Plan
Behavioral observation: frequency, duration, ABC recording
Documentation ethics: accuracy, timeliness, objectivity, avoiding speculation
Electronic health records: navigation and data entry protocols
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write a SOAP note from a role-played intake session

review.value: S: client's self-report and presenting concerns. O: observable behaviors, test results, clinician observations. A: clinical impressions, diagnostic considerations. P: treatment plan, next steps, referrals.
Document what happened, not what you think happened. Use behavioral descriptors ('client cried for 5 minutes') not judgments ('client was very upset')

class_schedule.topic: Integrating Theory and Practice: Case Conceptualization in the Field
learning_objectives: Apply case conceptualization frameworks to clients encountered at the field site
Discuss challenges of applying textbook knowledge to real-world complexity
Develop skills in presenting cases for supervision
readings: Eells, Handbook of Psychotherapy Case Formulation, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Real clients are messier than textbook cases. Learning to formulate complex presentations is a critical transition from student to professional.
key_concepts: Integrating biopsychosocial factors in real cases
Working with diagnostic uncertainty and comorbidity
Presenting a case in supervision: structured format (identifying info, presenting problem, history, formulation, plan, questions)
Adjusting conceptualization as new information emerges
Cultural humility in case conceptualization
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case presentation practice: students present anonymized cases from their field sites for group discussion

review.value: Demographics, referral source, presenting problem, relevant history, behavioral observations, diagnostic impressions, case formulation, treatment plan, supervision questions
In real practice, diagnosis is often provisional and evolves over time. Comfort with uncertainty is a sign of clinical maturity, not incompetence.

class_schedule.topic: Self-Care, Vicarious Trauma, and Professional Development
learning_objectives: Recognize signs of vicarious trauma and compassion fatigue
Develop a personal self-care plan grounded in research evidence
Discuss career paths in psychology and graduate school preparation

lecture_notes.introduction: Working with suffering individuals takes a toll. Sustainable professional practice requires deliberate attention to self-care and awareness of vicarious trauma.
key_concepts: Vicarious trauma: changes in worldview from exposure to clients' traumatic material
Compassion fatigue: emotional exhaustion from caring for suffering others
Burnout: emotional exhaustion, depersonalization, reduced accomplishment
Evidence-based self-care: physical activity, social connection, mindfulness, professional support
Career planning: graduate school paths (PhD, PsyD, MSW, MA), application timelines
lecture_notes.demonstration: Self-care assessment and planning exercise: identify current practices and gaps

review.value: Vicarious trauma is specifically related to exposure to traumatic content and changes in cognition (safety, trust, control). Burnout is broader exhaustion from any chronic occupational stress.
Stamm's Professional Quality of Life Scale: measures compassion satisfaction, burnout, and secondary traumatic stress. Regular self-monitoring is recommended.

learning_objectives: Integrate field experience with academic knowledge in a reflective synthesis
Evaluate personal and professional growth over the practicum
Develop a professional development plan for post-graduation

lecture_notes.introduction: Reflection transforms experience into learning. This final seminar synthesizes your field experience, identifies growth areas, and charts your path forward.
key_concepts: Reflective practice: reflecting on and in action (Schon)
Integration: connecting field observations to theories studied in coursework
Professional identity development: transitioning from student to professional
Ethical challenges encountered and how they were navigated
Post-graduation planning: applications, references, continuing education
lecture_notes.demonstration: Roundtable discussion: each student shares the most important lesson from their field experience

review.value: Schon: reflection-in-action (thinking while doing) and reflection-on-action (reviewing after the fact) are both essential for professional development
The transition from student to professional involves developing a personal therapeutic style, internalizing ethical standards, and accepting the weight of professional responsibility

major_assignments.title: Field Experience Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Compile a comprehensive portfolio documenting your field experience. Include weekly reflection journals, anonymized case presentations, documentation samples, supervisor evaluations, and a final integrative reflection paper connecting field experience to academic learning.
objectives: Document professional activities and learning throughout the field placement
Demonstrate ability to apply academic knowledge in applied settings
Reflect critically on professional development and areas for growth
Synthesize field experience with coursework into a coherent professional narrative
deliverables: Weekly reflection journals (12 entries, 300-500 words each)
Two anonymized case presentations with formulations
Site supervisor mid-term and final evaluations

criteria: Deep synthesis of field experience and academic knowledge
Honest self-assessment of strengths and growth areas
Articulates a clear post-graduation professional development plan
Evidence of growth from mid-term to final
Thorough documentation of field experience with consistent reflective journals
Professional case presentations demonstrating applied clinical knowledge
Deep integrative reflection connecting practice to theory
Positive supervisor evaluations demonstrating professional growth

field_experience_portfolio.description: Comprehensive documentation of the field placement experience

supervisor_evaluations.description: Mid-term and final evaluations by site supervisor

seminar_participation.description: Active engagement in weekly seminar discussions and case presentations

integrative_reflection_paper.description: Final synthesis paper connecting field experience to academic training

course_policies.attendance: Students must complete a minimum of 120 hours at the field site. Seminar attendance is mandatory. Absences from the field site must be reported to both the site supervisor and course instructor.
course_policies.late_submissions: Weekly journals must be submitted by the following Monday. Late journals will not receive written feedback. The final portfolio is penalized 5% per day late.
course_policies.collaboration: Case discussions in seminar are collaborative. All written reflections and case presentations must be independently authored. Confidentiality of client information is paramount.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All case material must be properly de-identified. Fabrication of field hours or clinical experiences will result in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to electronic health records at the field site (training provided), university LMS for journal submissions, and word processing software.
